translations.translation:
Lü Yi (Jiyang)
呂乂 (季陽)
Lived AD ?–251
aSanguozhiBiographyRecords of the Three Kingdoms [三國志]
Scroll 39 (Shu 9)
byZL181
onDecember 21st, 2023

Lü Yi, styledJiyang, was fromNanyang[南陽].
His father,[Lü] Chang, escorted his former leader,1Liu Yan, into Shu [蜀]. It happened that the royal roads were obstructed,2so he could not return.
[Lü] Yiwas orphaned when he was young. He enjoyed reading books and playing thezither.•••
Here, “former leader” [故將] refers to someone who was formerly a “commandery leader” [郡將], or aGrand Administrator.Liu Yanwas the former superior ofLü Chang, asLiu YanwasGrand AdministratorofNanyangin the past.↩
Here, “royal roads” [王路] refers to the main roads that led back to the imperial court.↩
Previously, when theFormer LordstabilizedYi province[益州], he installed aColonel of the Salt Bureau, who controlled profits of salt and iron. Later,ColonelWang Lianinvited[Lü] Yi,Du QiofNanyang[南陽], andLiu GanofNanxiang[南鄉], among others, to all serve asCommandants of the Directing Bureau.
[Lü] Yiwas promoted toPrefectofXindu[新都令] andPrefectofMianzhu[新都令]. His heart was compassionate and condolent, hence the common people praised him, being the most out of the various counties in theprovince. He was promoted toGrand AdministratorofBaxi[巴西太守].
WhenAssisting ChancellorZhuge Liangmarched out with the army for a few years, he mobilized and sent out several commanderies, but many of them did not reinforce.[Lü] Yienlisted five thousand people for[Zhuge] Liang, calming them with explanations and enacting restraints. Hence, there was no one who fled or scurried off. He was promoted toGrand AdministratorofHanzhong[漢中太守], and he also led asAgriculture Supervisor, supplying and maintaining the army provisions.
After[Zhuge] Liangdied, he was eventually promoted toGrand AdministratorofGuanghan[廣漢太守] andGrand AdministratorofShu commandery[蜀郡太守].Shu commandery[蜀郡太守] had the capital. Its households and people were numerous, and after[Zhuge] Liangdied, many soldiers and squads deserted and conveniently did multiple impersonations;3there was not just only one acting with cunning and guile. When[Lü] Yitook office, he enacted safeguards and prohibitions against such, and he made explanations, urgings, and persuasions. Within several years, those who were missing and came back on their own were over ten thousandpeople.•••
The deserted soldiers pretended to be common citizens of the populatedShu commandery. Some impersonated more than once in order to stay hidden.↩
He entered to serve asSecretariat Minister, replacingDong YunasDirector of the Secretariat. Of the various affairs, there were none that were delayed, and at the gates, there were no parked visitors.4[Lü] Yiheld successive offices in and out [of court]. He cultivated himself to be frugal and thrifty. He was modest and mindful, being taciturn. His governance was simple and not intrusive; he was called the “Pure and Capable”. However, he upheld the law very harshly, and he was fond of employing unrefined officials.5Thus, though he occupied a high office, his reputation was poorer than when he was in the commanderies andcounties.•••
This is saying thatLü Yiwas so efficient that there was no one waiting at the gates for their issues to be completed, forLü Yiwould complete them fast.↩
Here, “unrefined officials” [文俗吏] are officials perceived as being vulgar because they placed the law over morals, which was not considered Ruist.↩
In the fourteenth year of theera [251], he died. His son,[Lü] Chen, wasPrefectofChengdu[成都令] during theera. The younger brother of[Lü] Chen,[Lü] Ya, served asInternuncio.[Lü] Yawas pure, stern, and had literary talent. He authored thePrecise Discussion, which was fifteen sheets.
***
Du Qiserved asCommandery Administratorin different areas,Army Supervisor, andMajorunder theGrand General, while the rank ofLiu GanreachedGrand AdministratorofBaxi[巴西太守]. They were close and friendly with[Lü] Yi, and they were also praised at the time, but their frugality, simplicity, and adherence to the law could not reach those of[Lü] Yi.
